title: Equidistant resonance jumps in superconducting coplanar resonators driven by Abrikosov vortices
authors: Dmitrii S. Kalashnikov, Denis Yu. Vodolazov, Ruslan I. Kinzibaev, Andrei G. Shishkin, Vasily S. Stolyarov
abstract: Superconducting coplanar resonators are key building blocks of cryogenic microwave circuits, yet their performance in perpendicular magnetic fields is ultimately limited by Abrikosov vortices. In this work we investigate the dependence of the transmission parameter $S_{21}$ of niobium quarter-wave coplanar resonators on perpendicular magnetic fields up to 40 Oe and at temperatures between 18 mK and 5 K. Beyond the reversible Meissner regime, the entire resonance peak exhibits abrupt, staircase-like jumps as a function of magnetic field. Upon reversal of the field sweep, these jumps form an almost equidistant series with spacing 1.7-1.8 Oe, which, in agreement with theoretical estimates, we interpret as signatures of multiple-vortex entry and exit events. Additionally, we observe the non-proportional responses of the resonant frequency and the internal quality factor that indicate a complex contribution of vortex and antivortex configurations. We believe that our results will stimulate further studies of large vortex-antivortex systems, explicitly accounting for their discrete nature.
summary: This work experimentally probes the limitations of superconducting coplanar resonators due to Abrikosov vortices by measuring their microwave response under varying magnetic fields. The key finding is the observation of abrupt, equidistant jumps in the resonance frequency and quality factor, which are interpreted as signatures of quantized vortex entry and exit events. This research provides crucial insights into vortex dynamics for superconducting quantum circuit design.
main_problem: The study investigates how Abrikosov vortices limit the performance of superconducting coplanar resonators when subjected to perpendicular magnetic fields.
main_result: The resonance peak exhibits abrupt, staircase-like jumps as the magnetic field changes, with field reversals showing an almost equidistant series of jumps interpreted as multiple vortex entry/exit events.
method: High-resolution magnetospectroscopy was performed by measuring the complex transmission coefficient $S_{21}$ of niobium resonators across varying magnetic fields and temperatures.
model_or_system: The physical system is a superconducting coplanar resonator patterned on niobium films. The analysis models the response based on vortex dynamics, comparing observations to theoretical estimates derived from critical-state models.
key_observables: Resonant frequency ($f_r$), internal quality factor ($Q_i$), and the discrete jump spacing ($\Delta H \approx 1.7-1.8 	ext{ Oe}$).
important_parameters: Perpendicular magnetic field ($H_0$) up to 40 Oe, temperatures between 18 mK and 5 K.
main_assumptions: The jumps are attributed to discrete vortex entry/exit events, and the non-proportional response of $f_r$ and $Q_i$ suggests complex vortex/antivortex contributions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the chip layout, the magnetic-field dependence of $f_r$ and $Q_i$ across a full hysteresis cycle, and plots showing the linear relationship between jump field and jump number.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the resonator performance in the Meissner regime to observing the abrupt, staircase-like jumps upon vortex penetration, detailing the field reversal behavior and comparing results between different sample qualities.
why_interesting: The discrete, quantized nature of the resonance jumps provides a direct, measurable signature of the underlying quantum mechanical process of vortex flux penetration and dynamics in a superconducting circuit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sample A. (a) Design of the chip layout with six capacitively coupled (notch-type) quarter-wave coplanar res- onators. The inset shows optical image of the resonator sec- tion. (b) Broadband transmission S21(f) showing six reso- nance dips. (c,d) Representative resonance dips for resonators #1 and #3 together with fits by an asymmetric Lorentzian line shape [33].

FIG. 2. Magnetic-field dependence of resonator #3. (a,b) Color maps of the transmission amplitude |S21| as a function of the probe frequency and the applied perpendicular field. The frequency axis is plotted relative to the zero-field resonance f0 = 3.832 GHz (horizontal gray dashed line). Resonance frequency fr (c,d) and internal quality factor Qi (e,f) extracted from line-shape fits during a full hysteresis cycle: sweeping from 0 to −Hmax, then to +Hmax, and back to 0. Arrows and colors indicate the sweep direction starting with the red curves. The color maps in (a,b) correspond to the orange lines shown in the subsequent panels. The black dashed curve is a parabolic fit to the low-field...

FIG. 3. Equidistant jumps in the resonance frequency. Panels (a) and (b) show the dependence of the resonant frequency on the magnetic field for the fundamental (λ/4) mode of resonators #1 and #3 correspondingly. Panels (c) and (d) present behavior of the additional inductance in magnetic field calculated from the frequency change. Arrows indicate the magnetic field sweep direction. The data in panel (b) correspond to a segment of the plot in Fig. 2 (d). Color-coded markers denote data points following a sharp frequency jump. Panel (e) plots, using markers, the magnetic field value at which a frequency jump occurs versus the jump sequence number. Dashed lines represent linear fits to the...

FIG. 4. Field-induced evolution of the resonance peak at different temperatures. Each panel shows S21 (dB) as a function of probe frequency and the applied field swept from 0 to 10 Oe. The top row corresponds to sample A, and the bottom row to sample B; the resonators have identical geometries (Res #3) but are fabricated on different substrates. All panels use the same axis ranges (6 MHz along the frequency axis and 10 Oe along the field axis). The field is swept from 0 toward positive values. Before each temperature point, the sample is warmed above Tc to remove trapped flux and then cooled at zero field.

FIG. 5. (a) Field-dependent kinetic inductance of a vortex (Lv k, solid symbols) and of a narrow strip without a vortex (LM k , open symbols) obtained using the Ginzburg-Landau ap- proach. In the inset we show sketch of the strip with a vortex. (b) Field-dependent kinetic inductance of a vortex pinned in the center of the superconducting strip for different pinning strengths, controlled by the parameter a in the Eq. (B1).
